sessions.set_the_stage:
If you walked in with an idea already, skip ahead to Session 1. If you did not, take twenty to thirty minutes with this short pre-session and you will leave with one.

The biggest myth about entrepreneurship is that you need a flash of genius before you can start. You do not. You need a problem you actually care about, and a small honest description of who has it. That is all. The idea itself almost always comes out of a problem someone you can picture is dealing with.

So instead of trying to think of an idea, you are going to use Gemini to surface problems that match your interests, your life, and the kinds of people you already understand. Then you will pick one that feels alive to you and bring that into Session 1.

questions.title: Whose problems do you already understand?
questions.body: Not who you want to help. Who do you already know. Write down three groups of people whose daily lives you can describe in some detail. Could be students at your college, freelance designers, parents of toddlers, people who run small kirana stores, weekend cyclists, anyone.

questions.title: What annoys you, personally?
questions.body: List five things that have made you genuinely irritated in the last month. Tiny annoyances count. Forgetting where you parked. Spending an hour comparing three insurance policies. Not being able to find someone to play badminton with on a Sunday morning.

questions.title: What do you keep wishing existed?
questions.body: Sometimes the cleanest signal is the phrase someone wishes existed. Write down two or three things you have thought I wish there was a thing that did this. Do not filter for whether it could exist or already does. Just write what came to mind.

prompts.title: Find your idea
prompts.content:
I am about to spend two days building a real AI-powered product as a solopreneur. I do not have an idea yet. Help me find one.

Here is what I know about myself.

Three groups of people whose lives I understand: [from Question 1]
Five things that have annoyed me in the last month: [from Question 2]
Two or three things I wish existed: [from Question 3]

Do four things for me.

First, look at the annoyances and the wishes. Tell me which two or three of them point to a real problem that other people probably also experience. Not just me. Be honest if any of them are too personal or too niche to be a real micro-venture.

Second, for each of those candidate problems, describe in two sentences who specifically has this problem. The more specific the better. Not people or professionals. A specific kind of person in a specific situation.

Third, for each candidate, name an existing tool or workaround people currently use, and one thing about that workaround that is genuinely not good enough.

Fourth, recommend the one problem out of these candidates that you think is most worth me exploring this weekend, and tell me honestly why. Then ask me three follow-up questions to help me get sharper on that problem before I move to Session 1.

build.title: Read Gemini's response
build.instructions: Pay attention to the candidate it recommends, and also pay attention to which one made you sit up a little when you read it. Sometimes Gemini's pick is right. Sometimes your gut is right. Trust your gut if it disagrees, but only if you can explain why.

build.title: Have a second round
build.instructions: Answer Gemini's three follow-up questions. Push back when its picks feel off. One or two more turns of back-and-forth is usually enough.

build.title: Write your card
build.instructions: By the end, you should be able to write three things on a card. One sentence about the problem. Who specifically has it. Why their current workaround is not good enough.

test_output.title: The problem test
test_output.description: Can you say the problem in one sentence without mentioning your solution? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The person test
test_output.description: Can you describe one specific kind of person who has this problem, in enough detail that a stranger in the room could recognise them? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The gap test
test_output.description: Can you name what people currently do about this problem, and explain why it is not good enough? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The energy test
test_output.description: Does this idea make you a little excited to spend two days on it? If yes, pass. If no, run the prompt again with a different framing.

sessions.title: Do you have an idea yet?

sessions.set_the_stage:
You walked in with an idea. Maybe one you have been carrying for months. Maybe one that came to you last week. Maybe one you found in pre-session 0 this morning. Whichever it is, right now it lives only in your head, and right now nobody has tested whether it would survive contact with reality.

This session does that test. By the end of ninety minutes, the idea you bring in will either be sharper than it was when you arrived, or you will have replaced it with something stronger. Either is a good outcome.

There is no shame in pivoting at this stage. The shame is in building for two days on top of an idea that you privately knew was thin.

questions.title: What is the idea?
questions.body: Write your idea on a card. Then look at it. Is what you wrote an idea, or is it a solution? Most people skip straight to the solution. Push yourself back to the problem. What is the actual problem the world has, separate from your idea for solving it?

questions.title: Is this a real problem?
questions.body: Three levels of reality. Level one, you have personally felt this pain yourself. That is the strongest signal. Level two, you have watched someone you know struggle with it. That is a strong signal. Level three, you think it probably exists out there. That is the weakest signal and needs validation before you build anything.

questions.title: Who specifically has this problem?
questions.body: Not everyone. Not professionals. Not students. One specific kind of person in one specific situation. The test is whether you could recognise this person at a party. If yes, specific enough. If no, keep going.

questions.title: How often does this problem occur?
questions.body: Daily, weekly, monthly, occasionally? Frequency alone does not determine value. Frequency multiplied by cost does.

questions.title: What does it cost them?
questions.body: Not just time or money. What does it cost them emotionally? What do they feel when this problem appears? What do they miss out on because it exists? The emotional cost is what turns a curious person into a paying customer.

questions.title: What have they already tried?
questions.body: If this problem is real, people have tried to solve it. What have they tried? What tools, workarounds, or habits do they use today? Why is that not good enough? This question reveals both your competition and the gap, in one shot.

questions.title: Would they pay for a solution?
questions.body: If a solution existed that worked exactly as promised, what would it be worth to this person? Willingness to pay is not really about price. It is about whether the pain is acute enough.

techniques: Picture the specific person with the problem. Where are they? What time of day is it? What just happened?
What are they feeling in that moment? Frustration, embarrassment, exhaustion, resignation?
Could the room recognise this person if they walked in?
Is AI genuinely useful here, or are you forcing it?
Watch for the AI-for-the-sake-of-AI idea, where you find a problem to justify the tech.
Watch for the spreadsheet problem, where a form would solve it just as well.
Does someone currently pay money to have this problem managed?
Does someone currently spend significant time on it?
Does someone currently experience a painful consequence when it is not solved?

prompts.title: Stress test your idea
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur exploring an idea for a new micro-venture. I need you to stress test it honestly.

My idea: [one sentence]
My target user: [specific person in specific situation]
The problem they experience: [specific problem, not the solution]
How often they experience it: [frequency]
What it costs them: [time, money, emotional cost]
What they currently do about it: [existing workaround]
Why the workaround is not good enough: [the gap]

Run this through three lenses and be direct.

Humanics. Is my target user specific enough? Is the problem genuinely felt by a real human or am I building for a demographic I imagine?

Mechanics. Is AI genuinely useful here? Should AI solve this or am I forcing it? Name the failure mode if you see one.

Valuenomics. Is there a real value exchange? Would this specific person pay for a solution?

End with three hard questions I should be able to answer before I start building.

prompts.title: Validate the problem
prompts.content:
Find evidence that the following problem is real.

Problem: [their problem statement]
Target user: [specific person and situation]

Search for Reddit discussions, LinkedIn posts, forum threads, news articles, and research where real people describe experiencing this exact frustration. Give me five specific examples with quotes where possible. Tell me how widespread this problem appears to be and whether any existing solutions are addressing it well.

build.title: Run both prompts at the same time
build.instructions: Open Gemini in one tab, Perplexity in another. Paste the first prompt into Gemini, the second into Perplexity. Let them work in parallel for ten to fifteen minutes.

build.title: Answer Gemini's three hard questions honestly
build.instructions: Some of your answers will reveal weakness in the idea. Strengthen it, or pivot to a better version. This is not failure. This is the session working as designed.

build.title: Read what Perplexity found
build.instructions: Real people describing real pain in their own words. Pay attention to the exact language they use. That language belongs in your website, your chatbot, and your video later.

test_output.title: The specificity test
test_output.description: Can you describe your target user in enough detail that someone in this room could recognise them on the street? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The reality test
test_output.description: Can you name one specific real person, not a friend, who has this problem right now? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The pain test
test_output.description: Can you describe what your target user feels emotionally when this problem occurs? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The gap test
test_output.description: Can you explain specifically why existing solutions are not good enough? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The value test
test_output.description: Can you name one specific reason this person would pay for a solution? If yes, pass.

sessions.title: Ideation

sessions.set_the_stage:
You now have a real problem with a real human at its centre. Most people at this point make one mistake. They open a tool and start building immediately. They figure out what they are building as they go.

That is how you waste four hours and end up with something that does not work.

A solopreneur who has built before does something different. They spend thirty minutes designing before they touch a single tool. Not a detailed specification. Not a project plan. Just enough clarity to know exactly what they are building before they start.

Three things. What is the simplest possible version of the solution? What does the user put in and what do they get out? What does the AI do and what does it never do? Answer those in the next thirty minutes and you will build twice as fast and twice as well.

questions.title: What is the simplest possible version?
questions.body: Describe your solution in one sentence. Not the full vision. Not the ideal. The minimum version that still creates real value. Push until it is one thing that does one job well.

questions.title: What does the user put in?
questions.body: What is the minimum information your user needs to provide for the solution to work? Every field is friction. Friction reduces completion. What is the absolute minimum?

questions.title: What does the user get out?
questions.body: What exactly appears when the solution runs? The output format must serve the user, not you. A busy executive needs something they can read in two minutes. A student needs something that explains its reasoning.

questions.title: What does the AI do at each step?
questions.body: Walk through what the AI is actually doing. Step one, it receives this. Step two, it does this. Step three, it produces this. Push back against magical thinking. If you cannot explain what it is doing, neither can it.

questions.title: Where does the human step in?
questions.body: At what point does a human need to review the AI output before it reaches the user? Every AI product has a handoff point. Design it deliberately, not by accident.

questions.title: What should the AI never do?
questions.body: Name three things your AI must never do, regardless of what the user asks. Constraints are not limitations. They build trust.

techniques: Would your target user immediately understand what to do?
At what moment does the user feel genuine relief or satisfaction?
At what moment could the user feel uncertain? Design to address it.
Can the AI produce consistently good output for the full range of inputs?
What happens when the AI gets it wrong? Is it recoverable?
Are the constraints specific enough to actually constrain behaviour?
Does the user immediately understand what they received and why it is valuable?
Does the output tell the user what to do next?
Would a user look at this output and feel it was worth the effort?

prompts.title: Define the minimum solution
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur designing my first AI-powered product. I need to define the simplest possible version that still creates real value.

My problem statement: [from Session 1]
My target user: [from Session 1]
My value proposition: [from Session 1]

Help me define the minimum viable version of this solution. Then give me the solution in one sentence and the three-step user flow. Step 1, the user inputs [what]. Step 2, the AI does [what]. Step 3, the user receives [what in what format].

Be specific. Be simple. Push back if I am making this more complex than it needs to be.

prompts.title: Write the system prompt
prompts.content:
I am building the following AI-powered product.

Solution in one sentence: [from Prompt 1]
Target user: [from Session 1]
What the user inputs: [from Prompt 1]
What the AI produces: [from Prompt 1]
Three things the AI must never do: [from Question 6]

Write me a system prompt with seven elements.

Role, who the AI is and what it exists to do.
Context, what the AI needs to know about the situation.
Instructions, step by step what the AI does when triggered.
Tone, how the AI communicates.
Output format, exactly how the AI structures its output.
Constraints, the three things the AI never does.
Escalation, when the AI should stop and tell the user to seek human help.

prompts.title: Test the system prompt
prompts.content:
Read the system prompt you just wrote. Now play the role of my target user.

My target user is: [human portrait]

Give me three inputs this user might realistically provide. For each input, run the system prompt mentally and tell me what output it would produce. Is that output genuinely useful?

Then give me one edge case input. What happens? Does the system prompt handle it gracefully or does it break?

build.title: Three prompts in sequence
build.instructions: Run each prompt one at a time in the same Claude conversation. About fifteen minutes total.

build.title: Read every line of the system prompt
build.instructions: Do not skim. Each of the seven elements affects how your AI will behave with real users. Edit anything that does not match your design.

build.title: Fix the edge case if it breaks
build.instructions: If Prompt 3 reveals the system prompt fails on the edge case, ask Claude to rewrite the affected element. Test again.

test_output.title: The simplicity test
test_output.description: Can you explain what your product does in one sentence?

test_output.title: The flow test
test_output.description: Can you describe the three-step user flow without hesitation?

test_output.title: The system prompt test
test_output.description: Does your system prompt have all seven elements?

test_output.title: The edge case test
test_output.description: Has your system prompt been tested against at least one unexpected input?

test_output.title: The HMV test
test_output.description: Can you answer all three HMV questions without hesitation?

sessions.title: Design
sessions.break_note: After lunch, you build your AI App. Show up fresh.

sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a problem. You have a design. You have a system prompt. Now you build.

Today, a solopreneur with a clear idea and the right prompts can have a working product with a live URL in ninety minutes. Not a mockup. Not a wireframe. Something real that a stranger can open on their phone right now.

One HTML file. No code knowledge required. No API key. No backend. Everything runs in the browser. You build it with Claude, customise it until it feels like yours, and deploy it to the internet before this session ends.

questions.title: What does the user see first?
questions.body: When your target user opens your AI App for the first time, what is the first impression? Not the design. The feeling. Does that feeling match the problem you are solving?

questions.title: What is the app called and what does it say it does?
questions.body: Your AI App needs a name and a one-line description. The name should say something true. The description should tell the user exactly what they will get. There is a difference between a name that sounds impressive and a name that actually informs.

questions.title: How does the user interact with it?
questions.body: Walk through the user's physical interaction. What do they click? What do they type? What do they see changing on screen? Every extra click is friction. Every unclear label is friction.

questions.title: What does the output look like?
questions.body: When the AI App produces its result, what does the user actually see? A wall of text? A structured plan? A numbered list? A scorecard? Connect the output format to your user. A busy professional cannot read a wall of text.

questions.title: What makes it feel trustworthy?
questions.body: What specific element makes a sceptical user trust it enough to use the output? The three trust signals for AI products are specificity, clarity, and honesty. Which matters most for your specific user?

questions.title: What happens after the output?
questions.body: When the user has their result, what do you want them to do next? Every product has a natural next action. If the app does not guide the user toward it, most users will close the tab and the value will be lost.

techniques: Open the app as if you have never seen it. Is it immediately obvious what to do?
Read every label and button out loud. Does it sound like something a real person would say?
Would a real user complete the full journey without help?
Run the app with a real input, not a perfect test input. What happens?
Run the app with an input it was not designed for. Does it fail gracefully?
Run the same input twice. Do you get the same quality of output?
Look at the output for three seconds and look away. What did you retain?
If someone paid for this output, would they feel it was worth it?
Would your target user screenshot the output and send it to a colleague?

prompts.title: Build the AI App
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur building my first AI-powered product. I need you to build me a complete single-page HTML app.

App name: [from Question 2]
One-line description: [from Question 2]
Target user: [from Session 1]
First impression feeling: [from Question 1]
What the user inputs: [from Session 2]
How the user interacts: [from Question 3]
What the output looks like: [from Question 4]
What makes it trustworthy: [from Question 5]
What happens after the output: [from Question 6]
System prompt for the logic: [from Session 2]

Build me a complete single-page HTML app. No external API calls. Everything runs in the browser. The design should feel professional and trustworthy. After you build it, explain each section in plain language so I understand exactly what you built and why. I will read it carefully and ask you to make specific changes.

prompts.title: Refine and personalise
prompts.content:
Read the HTML app you just built. I want to make the following specific changes.

Change 1: [specific change]
Change 2: [specific change]
Change 3: [specific change]

Make these changes and show me the updated file. Explain what you changed and why the change improves the app for my target user.

build.title: Stage 1. Claude builds the app (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Paste Prompt 1 into Claude. Claude produces the complete HTML file with a plain-language explanation of every section. Read every section carefully. Do not rush. Understand what each part does before moving on.

build.title: Stage 2. You direct the changes (20 minutes)
build.instructions: Identify two or three specific things to change. Not vague feedback. Specific changes. Paste Prompt 2 with your specific changes. Claude updates the file. Review again.

build.title: Stage 3. Deploy to Netlify (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Copy Claude's HTML output. Open a text editor (TextEdit on Mac, Notepad on Windows, or VS Code if you have it). Paste the HTML. Save the file as index.html on your desktop. Open netlify.com in a new tab and sign in. Look for the drag-and-drop area on your dashboard. Drag your index.html file into it. Wait two minutes. Netlify generates a live URL. Open it on your phone and test with one real input.

build.title: Stage 4. Iterate once
build.instructions: Identify one thing that needs improving. Update the HTML in Claude. Save the new file as index.html (replacing the old one). Drag it back into Netlify to redeploy. Test on phone again.

test_output.title: The live URL test
test_output.description: Your AI App has a live URL that works on a phone browser without any explanation from you.

test_output.title: The stranger test
test_output.description: Open the app as if you have never seen it. It is immediately obvious what to do without reading any instructions.

test_output.title: The real input test
test_output.description: Run the app with a real input your target user would actually provide. The output serves them genuinely.

test_output.title: The trust test
test_output.description: The output looks like something a professional produced, not raw AI output.

test_output.title: The worth test
test_output.description: Your target user would feel the output was worth the time they spent providing the input.

output_capture.ask: Paste the live URL of your AI App.
output_capture.guidance: This is the URL you got from Netlify after dragging your index.html into it. It should open on any phone without needing a login.

sessions.title: Build the AI App
sessions.break_note: Stretch, water, fresh air. Back in fifteen.

sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a product. It works. It lives at a real URL. But right now, nobody knows it exists.

A solopreneur without a website is a shopkeeper without a shopfront. Your website is a conversation with a stranger who has never heard of you. A conversation that has to answer three questions in under thirty seconds. What is this? Is this for me? What do I do next?

If your website answers those three questions clearly, the stranger clicks through to your AI App. If it does not, they leave. You have sixty minutes to build that conversation.

questions.title: What is the one thing your website must make a stranger feel?
questions.body: Not what it must say. What it must make them feel. In the first three seconds before they have read a single word. Connect the feeling to the human portrait.

questions.title: What is the headline?
questions.body: One line. Maximum ten words. Product headlines describe what the product is. User headlines describe what changes for the user. Which type is yours? If it is a product headline, rewrite it as a user headline.

questions.title: Why would your target user hesitate?
questions.body: Before clicking and trying your app, what is the one thing that makes them pause? The three most common hesitations for AI products are whether it will work for their specific situation, whether they can trust the output, and whether it will be complicated.

questions.title: What is the one action you want them to take?
questions.body: Not two actions. Not a menu of options. One. What is the single most important thing a visitor should do? Almost always, the answer is try the AI App.

questions.title: Why should a stranger trust you?
questions.body: The more specific you are about who this is for, what it does, and what it does not do, the more a specific user trusts you. Generic claims create generic trust, which is no trust at all.

questions.title: How does it work in three steps?
questions.body: Describe how your product works in three steps. One sentence each. Simple enough that a person who has never used AI can understand it immediately. Every piece of jargon is a barrier.

techniques: Count you versus I or we. You should appear far more than I or we.
Would your target user read the headline and think this is written about me?
Read the copy out loud as if speaking directly to your target user.
Does the website describe what the product actually does, not what you wish it did?
Does the how-it-works section describe what the AI does in plain language?
After reading, does the visitor have an accurate expectation of what will happen?
After thirty seconds, can someone say what the product does and who it is for?
Does the website name a specific outcome?
Does the website help the visitor understand why this is better than their current workaround?

prompts.title: Write the website copy
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur building a marketing website for my product. Write the copy for six sections. Final words ready to paste. No explanations. Just the best version.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My value proposition: [from Session 1]
The feeling the website must create: [from Question 1]
My AI App URL: [Netlify URL]
My target user's primary hesitation: [from Question 3]

Section 1, Headline. One line. Maximum ten words. A user headline, not a product headline.
Section 2, Subheadline. Two sentences. Who this is for. What specifically changes.
Section 3, How it works. Three steps. One sentence each. No jargon.
Section 4, Trust statement. Directly addresses the primary hesitation. Honest. Specific.
Section 5, Call to action. One button label that describes the action and hints at value.
Section 6, Credibility line. One sentence. Why I am the right person to have built this.

prompts.title: Test and refine
prompts.content:
Read the copy you just wrote as if you are my target user seeing this for the first time.

My target user is: [human portrait]

Ask yourself three questions. Does the headline stop me and make me think this is for me? Does the copy speak to me directly or could it apply to anyone? After reading everything, do I know exactly what to do next and do I want to do it?

For any section where the answer is no, rewrite that section. Tell me what you changed and why.

build.title: Stage 1. ChatGPT writes the copy (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Paste Prompt 1. ChatGPT produces all six sections. Read every section against the questions. Identify any section that feels generic or off-tone. Run Prompt 2 to refine.

build.title: Stage 2. Build the site on Carrd (40 minutes), step by step
build.instructions: 1. Open carrd.co and sign in.  2. Click Build a Site, then Start from scratch.  3. Pick the simplest single-column template. You can change the look later.  4. You will land in the editor. The page is on the left, controls on the right when you click any element.  5. Click the top text area. Replace the placeholder headline with your ChatGPT headline. Set a clear large size.  6. Below the headline, add or edit a smaller text area for your subheadline. Paste the two sentences from ChatGPT.  7. Click the plus icon to add a new section. Add a heading How it works, then paste the three steps below it.  8. Add another section for your Trust statement.  9. Add a button element. Type the call-to-action label. In the link field, paste your Netlify AI App URL. Set it to open in a new tab.  10. Add a small Credibility line at the bottom.  11. Add an Image element. Upload a screenshot of your AI App showing a real output.  12. Click the eye icon to preview the site.  13. Click Publish. Pick a name like yourname-product. Site lives at that-name.carrd.co.  14. Open the URL on your phone. Tap the button. Confirm it opens your AI App.  15. If anything looks off, edit and republish.

test_output.title: The thirty-second test
test_output.description: Show the website to someone in the room. After thirty seconds, they can say what this does and who it is for.

test_output.title: The pronoun test
test_output.description: Count you versus I or we. You appears significantly more.

test_output.title: The hesitation test
test_output.description: The website directly addresses the primary hesitation from Question 3.

test_output.title: The link test
test_output.description: The CTA button works and links to your AI App. You confirmed this on a phone.

test_output.title: The expectation test
test_output.description: After reading the website, a visitor has an accurate expectation of what will happen when they try the AI App.

output_capture.ask: Paste the live URL of your Carrd website.
output_capture.guidance: This is the published Carrd URL, not the editor URL. Open it on your phone first to confirm it works.

sessions.title: Build the Website

sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a product. You have a website. But not everyone who lands on your website is ready to try your product immediately. Some have questions. Some have doubts.

A chatbot is your always-on conversation partner. It handles the questions while you sleep. It addresses the doubts before they become reasons to leave. It guides the curious visitor toward becoming an actual user.

A good chatbot does not feel like a robot. It feels like a knowledgeable, helpful person who genuinely understands the product and cares about the visitor.

questions.title: What are the three questions your target user most wants answered?
questions.body: Not the questions you want to answer. The questions they actually have. The ones that sit between them reading your website and clicking the button. User questions are almost always about trust, time, and specificity.

questions.title: What does your chatbot sound like?
questions.body: Your chatbot has a personality. Describe it in three words. Connect the personality to the human portrait. A tool for anxious founders needs a warm and reassuring chatbot. A tool for experienced consultants needs a sharp and direct one.

questions.title: What is the chatbot's opening line?
questions.body: The first thing a visitor reads when the chatbot appears. It must make them feel that engaging is worth it. Does it acknowledge what the visitor is here for? Does it invite a natural response?

questions.title: What happens when the chatbot does not know the answer?
questions.body: Every chatbot reaches the edge of its knowledge. Failure mode one is making something up, which destroys trust instantly. Failure mode two is saying I do not know and stopping, which leaves the visitor stranded. The right response acknowledges the limitation and offers a clear alternative.

questions.title: How does every conversation end?
questions.body: Regardless of what question a visitor asks, every conversation should end with one thing. The job of the chatbot is to move the visitor one step closer to trying the AI App. What is that closing invitation for your specific product?

questions.title: What should the chatbot never say?
questions.body: Name two or three things that would make your target user immediately distrust or disengage. The three most common chatbot trust-breakers are overclaiming, jargon, and deflection.

techniques: Read every response out loud. Does it sound like a real person or like a FAQ page?
Does the chatbot acknowledge what the visitor is feeling before answering?
Every response should be shorter than it could be. Visitors scan, they do not read.
Can a visitor navigate from the opening line to the call-to-action without hitting a dead end?
What happens when a visitor says something completely unexpected?
Can the visitor ask a follow-up question after receiving an answer?
Does every conversation end with a genuine invitation to try the AI App?
Does the chatbot help visitors understand if the product is right for them?
After a conversation, does the visitor trust the product more than before?

prompts.title: Design the conversation flow
prompts.content:
I am building a chatbot for my solopreneur product using Voiceflow. Design the complete conversation flow.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
Chatbot personality in three words: [from Question 2]
The three questions my user most wants answered: [from Question 1]
What the chatbot never says: [from Question 6]
How every conversation ends: [from Question 5]
What happens when the chatbot does not know: [from Question 4]

Design the complete conversation flow structured as follows.

Opening message, acknowledges what the visitor is here for, invites a specific response.
Three question and answer pairs, for each of the three user questions. Each answer honest, specific, brief.
One edge case, a question the chatbot was not designed to handle. Show the honest response.
Closing invitation, the natural, genuine invitation to try the product that ends every conversation.

Format as a clear conversation script I can build in Voiceflow.

prompts.title: Test the conversation
prompts.content:
Read the conversation flow you just designed. Now play the role of three different visitors.

Visitor 1, a curious first-time visitor who has one specific question about whether this works for their situation.
Visitor 2, a sceptical visitor who pushes back on one of the answers and asks a follow-up question the chatbot was not designed for.
Visitor 3, a visitor who clicks through every option just to see what happens.

For each visitor, tell me honestly whether the chatbot served them well. Where does it break? Where does it lose them? What needs to change?

build.title: Stage 1. Mistral designs the conversation (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Paste Prompt 1. Read the full script out loud quietly to yourself. Reading aloud is the fastest way to catch unnatural language. Run Prompt 2 to stress-test the script against three visitor types.

build.title: Stage 2. Build in Voiceflow (35 minutes), step by step
build.instructions: 1. Open voiceflow.com and sign in.  2. Click Create New Project. Choose the Chatbot template (not Voice Assistant).  3. Name the project after your product. Click Create.  4. You will land in the canvas. The left panel has blocks you can drag in. The canvas is where you connect them.  5. The project starts with a Start block. Drag a Text block next to it. Connect them.  6. Click the Text block. On the right, paste your opening message from Mistral.  7. Add a Buttons (Choice) block after the opening. Add three buttons, one for each user question.  8. For each button, drag a Text block. Connect the button to its Text block. Paste the corresponding answer.  9. After each answer, add another Buttons block with a Try it for myself option. Connect that to a final Text block with your closing invitation including your Carrd website link.  10. In project settings, find the No Match response. Paste your edge-case response there.  11. Click the play icon to test in the simulator. Click through every path. Type something off-script.  12. When the conversation works end to end, click Publish.  13. Copy the embed code snippet.  14. Open your Carrd site in editor mode. Add an Embed element. Paste the Voiceflow code. Republish.

build.title: Stage 3. Test on phone (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Open the Carrd website on your phone. The chatbot should appear as a floating widget. Go through every path. Fix one thing in Voiceflow. Republish. Test again.

test_output.title: The live test
test_output.description: Every conversation path reaches the closing invitation without hitting a dead end.

test_output.title: The natural language test
test_output.description: Every chatbot response sounds like a real, knowledgeable person.

test_output.title: The edge case test
test_output.description: Typing something unexpected gets a graceful, honest response with a clear alternative.

test_output.title: The trust test
test_output.description: After someone tries the chatbot, they trust the product more than before.

test_output.title: The conversion test
test_output.description: Every conversation, regardless of path, ends with a genuine invitation to try the AI App.

output_capture.ask: Paste your website URL again, now with the chatbot embedded.
output_capture.guidance: The chatbot lives on your Carrd site as an embedded widget. Confirm it appears when you open the URL on your phone.

sessions.title: Build the Chatbot

sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at what you have built today. A working AI App with a live URL. A website that markets it. A chatbot that handles the questions. Three things that did not exist at 10am this morning.

Tonight you have one task. Thirty minutes. You do it at home, on your phone, or wherever you end up.

You are going to give your product a voice. Not a logo. Not a colour scheme. A literal voice. Sixty seconds of audio that captures who you are, what problem you solve, and why someone should try what you built.

You will write the script tonight using Gemini. Tomorrow morning you will generate it using ElevenLabs and it will be live on your website before 11am.

questions.title: What is the one moment you want the listener to feel?
questions.body: Not what you want them to know. What you want them to feel. At the end of sixty seconds, what is the one emotion you want them to carry? Curiosity. Recognition. Confidence.

questions.title: Whose voice is this?
questions.body: Founder voice is personal, credible, builds connection. Works when the founder's story is relevant to the user's problem. Product voice is direct, efficient, focused on the user. Works when the problem is universally felt. Which serves your target user better?

questions.title: What is the problem in one sentence, spoken out loud?
questions.body: Say the problem your product solves out loud. Right now. Without reading anything. The spoken version is almost always better than the written version. Record that sentence into your phone. That is your audio opening.

questions.title: What does your product do, explained to a friend?
questions.body: Explain your product to the person sitting next to you as if you are telling a friend about something useful you discovered. Casual. No jargon. No pitch. That casual explanation is the middle section of your audio.

questions.title: What is the one thing you want the listener to do after sixty seconds?
questions.body: Not two things. One. An invitation makes someone want to do it. Not a call-to-action that tells them what to do. A call-to-action tells. An invitation makes them want.

prompts.title: Write the audio script (do this tonight)
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur building a sixty-second audio piece for my brand. This is the voice of my product.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My value proposition: [from Session 1]
Whose voice this is: [founder or product, from Question 2]
The problem in my own words: [what I said out loud in Question 3]
How I explained it to a friend: [the casual explanation from Question 4]
The one thing I want the listener to do: [from Question 5]
The feeling I want them to carry: [from Question 1]

Write me a sixty-second audio script structured in three beats.

Beat 1, fifteen seconds. The problem. In the words my target user would use. Specific. Felt. Not generic.
Beat 2, thirty seconds. The solution. Conversational. Clear. No jargon.
Beat 3, fifteen seconds. The invitation. Natural. Not a call-to-action.

Write for the ear. Short sentences. Conversational rhythm. Natural pause points marked with [pause].

After the script, tell me the total word count and approximate speaking time at a natural pace.

build.title: Tonight's homework
build.instructions: Open Gemini on your phone or laptop. Paste the prompt with your specifics filled in. Read the script out loud immediately. Mark any sentence that feels unnatural to say. Ask Gemini to rewrite that sentence. Read it again. Save the final script. Bring it tomorrow.

test_output.title: The natural speech test
test_output.description: You can read the script out loud naturally from start to finish without any sentence feeling forced.

test_output.title: The timing test
test_output.description: Reading at a natural pace, the script lands between fifty-five and sixty-five seconds.

sessions.title: Audio  /  overnight briefing

sessions.set_the_stage:
Last night you wrote something real. A script that captures your problem, your product, and your invitation in sixty seconds. This morning you give it a voice.

ElevenLabs is one of the most remarkable tools in the no-code AI landscape. You paste in text. You choose a voice. You press generate. In thirty seconds you have professional-quality audio that sounds like it was recorded in a studio.

Ten minutes to generate. Ten minutes to embed. Ten minutes to test. By 11am your website has a voice.

questions.title: What does your brand sound like?
questions.body: Name a public figure, a podcaster, a presenter, or a type of voice that matches how you want your brand to sound. This primes your ear before you open ElevenLabs.

questions.title: Where on your website does the audio live?
questions.body: One specific place. The most effective placements are below the headline, after the visitor has read what the product is, or below the how-it-works section as a human confirmation of what they just learned.

build.title: Stage 1. Generate on ElevenLabs (10 minutes), step by step
build.instructions: 1. Open elevenlabs.io and sign in.  2. In the left menu, click Text to Speech.  3. Paste your script from last night into the large text area.  4. On the right, click the voice selector to browse the voice library.  5. Filter by language and accent. Listen to short samples of three or four voices. Pick the one matching the feeling from Question 1.  6. Below the voice, you will see Stability and Clarity sliders. Leave them at default for the first try.  7. Click Generate. Takes around thirty seconds.  8. Press play and listen to the entire sixty seconds. Identify one moment that sounds unnatural. Edit that part of the script and regenerate.  9. When happy, click Download. Save the MP3 as yourproduct-voice.mp3 to your desktop.

build.title: Stage 2. Embed on your website (10 minutes)
build.instructions: 1. Open your Carrd site in the editor.  2. Click the plus icon. Choose Audio.  3. Upload your MP3 from the desktop.  4. Position the audio element at the placement you decided in Question 2.  5. Set it to play on click, not autoplay. Auto-playing audio is intrusive.  6. Add a one-line label above it like Hear why I built this, sixty seconds.  7. Publish.

build.title: Stage 3. Test (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Open the website on your phone. Navigate to the audio element. Press play. Listen as your target user would. Identify one moment where attention drifts. Edit that part of the script, regenerate in ElevenLabs, swap the file in Carrd, republish. Test again.

test_output.title: The natural speech test
test_output.description: Every sentence sounds like something a real person would genuinely say.

test_output.title: The timing test
test_output.description: The audio lands between fifty-five and sixty-five seconds at a natural pace.

test_output.title: The live embed test
test_output.description: On a phone, the audio element plays when clicked.

test_output.title: The retention test
test_output.description: After listening once, you can name the problem, the solution, and the next step without looking at the script.

test_output.title: The stranger test
test_output.description: Play the audio to someone in the room who has not heard it. They can describe what the product does.

output_capture.ask: Paste your website URL again, now with the audio embedded.
output_capture.guidance: Your sixty seconds of audio lives on your Carrd site. Confirm it plays when you press the play icon on your phone.

sessions.title: Build the Audio

sessions.set_the_stage:
You have an AI App. A website. A chatbot. An audio piece. Now you need video.

Text tells someone what you built. Audio lets them hear your conviction. Video shows them the product working in real time. No description. No imagination required. They see it happen.

A ninety-second screen recording of your product doing its job, with you narrating over it, is the most powerful piece of content you will produce today. It travels. People share videos.

Production quality does not matter. What matters is authenticity. A polished corporate video creates distance. A real person showing a real product doing something genuinely useful creates connection.

questions.title: What is the most impressive moment in your product?
questions.body: If you could show a sceptical person one thing, what would it be? The most impressive moment is almost never the homepage. It is the output. The moment the result appears.

questions.title: What does the viewer need to believe before they see that moment?
questions.body: Before the impressive moment lands, what does the viewer need to understand or feel? The setup is showing the viewer that the problem is real, the current solution is inadequate, and what they are about to see addresses both.

questions.title: What do you say while the screen is showing?
questions.body: Not a script yet. Just the feeling. Excited? Calm and confident? Quietly proud? Conversational? The tone of narration should match what your target user needs to feel.

questions.title: What is the one sentence that describes the problem?
questions.body: Say it out loud. Right now. The problem your product solves in one sentence spoken the way you would say it to a friend. This sentence opens the video.

questions.title: Where does this video live after you record it?
questions.body: Not just the website. Where else? LinkedIn? WhatsApp? An email to a potential user? Name the specific places before you record.

questions.title: What do you want the viewer to do in the thirty seconds after the video ends?
questions.body: Not eventually. In the thirty seconds immediately after, while the impression is still fresh, what one action do you want them to take?

techniques: Watch the video back with the sound off. Does the narrator look like someone who genuinely cares?
Is the narration rushing? Slow down. Pause after the impressive moment.
Does the video show a real input from a real user, even if that user is you?
Is the text on screen large enough to read on a phone? Zoom your browser to 125 percent before recording.
Run through the demo once before recording. Make sure the product works exactly as expected.
Is the screen clean? Notifications off? Browser tabs minimised?
Does the video spend more time showing the product working than talking about it?
Does the video show the output in enough detail that the viewer can evaluate its quality?
After watching, is the next step obvious and accessible?

prompts.title: Write the narration script
prompts.content:
I am recording a ninety-second screen recording demo of my product using Loom. Write me the narration script.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
The problem in one sentence spoken: [from Question 4]
The most impressive moment in my product: [from Question 1]
What the viewer needs to believe before that moment: [from Question 2]
Narration tone: [from Question 3]
What I want the viewer to do immediately after watching: [from Question 6]
Where the link to my product lives: [website URL]

Write the narration script in three beats.

Beat 1, thirty seconds. The problem. Spoken in the words my target user would use. Sets up the impressive moment.
Beat 2, forty-five seconds. The demonstration. What I am showing on screen. Written so it sounds natural when spoken over a live screen recording.
Beat 3, fifteen seconds. The invitation. Natural. Specific. Names the one action and where to take it.

Mark each beat clearly. Mark any moments where I should pause and let the screen speak for itself.

After the script, give me five specific things to show on screen during Beat 2 in the exact order to show them.

build.title: Stage 1. Prepare (10 minutes)
build.instructions: 1. Read the script out loud twice. Edit anything that feels unnatural to speak.  2. Do not memorise the script. Keep it visible on screen while you record.  3. Clean your browser. Close unnecessary tabs.  4. Zoom your browser to 125 percent so text reads clearly on phones.  5. Turn off notifications on your device.  6. Open your AI App URL. Prepare a real input you will type during the recording.

build.title: Stage 2. Record on Loom (20 minutes), step by step
build.instructions: 1. Open loom.com or the Loom desktop app or Chrome extension.  2. Click New Recording. Choose Screen and Camera. The camera shows your face in a small bubble.  3. Pick the screen or window you want to record. Pick your microphone.  4. Click Start. You get a three-second countdown.  5. Speak from the script. Do not read word for word. Speak naturally.  6. Move through the five things from the script during Beat 2.  7. Pause for three full seconds after the impressive moment. Let the output sit in silence.  8. End with your website URL spoken out loud.  9. Click Stop. Loom processes and gives you a shareable link.

build.title: Stage 3. Post and embed (30 minutes), step by step
build.instructions: 1. Watch the video back in Loom. If it is broadly OK, do not re-record. Authenticity beats polish.  2. Download the MP4 from the three-dot menu.  3. Go to LinkedIn. Upload the MP4 as a native video (native gets higher reach than external links).  4. In the caption, write the problem in two sentences, the invitation in one. Include your website URL.  5. Back in Loom, click Share, then Embed. Copy the embed code.  6. Open your Carrd site in the editor. Add an Embed element below the how-it-works section. Paste the Loom embed code.  7. Republish Carrd. Open the website on your phone. Confirm the video plays without leaving the site.

test_output.title: The live post test
test_output.description: The video is on LinkedIn with a caption. You confirmed the post is live and the video plays.

test_output.title: The embed test
test_output.description: On a phone, the video plays from the Carrd website without needing to leave the site.

test_output.title: The impressive moment test
test_output.description: The impressive moment appears clearly. You paused long enough for the viewer to absorb it.

test_output.title: The stranger test
test_output.description: Show the video to someone who has not seen your product. They describe what it does and what they would do next.

test_output.title: The action test
test_output.description: After the video ends, the next step is obvious and accessible on a phone.

output_capture.ask: Paste the LinkedIn post URL of your video.
output_capture.guidance: This is the URL of your LinkedIn post, not the Loom URL. Open the post in a fresh browser and confirm the video plays.

sessions.title: Build the Video
sessions.break_note: Use this lunch to call one real person who has the problem your product solves. Five questions, twenty minutes, verbatim notes.

sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at everything you have built. A product that works. A website that markets it. A chatbot that handles questions. An audio piece that gives it a voice. A video that shows it in action.

All of it is visible. All of it requires someone to find it, open it, and engage with it. Now you build the invisible layer. The thing that runs while you sleep. While you are in meetings. While you are building the next version.

That is what automation is. Something that takes one trigger and produces one action, reliably, repeatedly, without you.

We are not going near Make.com or Zapier today. They are powerful, but the learning curve is real, and forty-five minutes is not enough. Instead we are using Tally. A free form builder that comes with automation baked in. You set up a form. Someone fills it. They receive an automatic personalised email. You receive a notification. The submission is logged. That is a full automated workflow, and you will have it live before this session ends.

questions.title: What is the one thing you would most want to happen automatically?
questions.body: Not a list. One thing. The thing that, if it happened without you every time, would make the biggest difference. The most common solopreneur automations are: someone signs up for early access and gets a welcome message. Someone reports a problem and gets an acknowledgment. Someone asks to be added to a waitlist and gets a confirmation with what happens next.

questions.title: What is the one piece of information you need from them?
questions.body: Email, at minimum. Beyond that, what one or two things would let you respond personally? Their name. Their use case. Their biggest pain point. Pick at most three questions. More than that and people will not fill it in.

questions.title: What goes in the automated email back to them?
questions.body: Not a templated, robotic confirmation. A real message that sounds like you. What does the email need to say? Acknowledge their submission. Tell them what happens next. Set an expectation for when they hear from you again.

questions.title: Where on your website does the form live?
questions.body: Open your Carrd site. Where is the natural place to invite someone to submit? Below the AI App call-to-action? In a dedicated section called Get early access? Pick one specific spot.

questions.title: How do you know it is working?
questions.body: After you build this and turn it on, how do you know it fired? Tally sends you a notification of every submission. You also get the data in a Tally dashboard. The user gets their auto-confirmation email. Three confirmations, one workflow.

techniques: Does the auto-confirmation email feel written for this specific person or like a mass email with a name inserted?
Does the form ask the minimum needed, not the maximum possible?
Does the email sound like the same person who wrote the website copy and narrated the video?
Test the form yourself with a real submission before you embed it.
Does the confirmation email arrive within thirty seconds?
If a user submits with an empty field or weird data, does the form handle it gracefully?
How much time does this automation save per week? Each manual reply is two to three minutes.
Does this automation become more valuable as more people find your product?
Would your user feel valued by the response they receive, or would they feel processed?

prompts.title: Design the form and the auto-reply
prompts.content:
I am a solopreneur setting up a simple Tally form on my website with an auto-confirmation email. Help me design it.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My website URL: [from Session 4]
What I want the form to do: [from Question 1]
The information I need from them: [from Question 2]
What I want the email to convey: [from Question 3]

Do three things.

First, write me the form. Title, short description, and the two or three questions to ask. Make every question feel inviting, not bureaucratic.

Second, write the auto-confirmation email. Subject line plus body. Make it sound like a real person wrote it. Use the user's first name. Tell them what happens next. Set an honest expectation for when they will hear from me. Keep it under 120 words.

Third, write a one-line invitation that sits above the embedded form on my Carrd site. It should tell the visitor what they get by filling it in.

build.title: Stage 1.  Gemini drafts the form and email (5 minutes)
build.instructions: Paste the prompt. Read the form questions and the email out loud. Edit anything that does not sound like you. Save the final form questions and email body in a notes app for the next stages.

build.title: Stage 2.  Build the form on Tally (15 minutes), step by step
build.instructions: 1. Open tally.so and sign in. Free tier is fine.  2. Click New form. Pick Start from scratch.  3. Add a title block with your form title from Gemini.  4. Add a Short answer block for each question. The first one should always be Name. The second should always be Email.  5. Make Name and Email required. Other questions optional.  6. Click the settings icon (gear) for the form.  7. Go to the Email notifications section. Toggle on Send email to respondents.  8. Paste your auto-confirmation email subject line and body from Gemini. Use the personalisation tag for first name like {{Name}}.  9. In the same settings, toggle on Send email to me on every submission so you get notified.  10. Click Publish at the top right.  11. Tally gives you a Share link and an Embed code. Copy the embed code.

build.title: Stage 3.  Embed on your Carrd site (10 minutes), step by step
build.instructions: 1. Open your Carrd site in the editor.  2. Find the spot you decided on in Question 4.  3. Click the plus icon. Choose Embed.  4. Paste your Tally embed code. Tally embeds render natively.  5. Add a heading or one-line invitation above the form using your line from Gemini.  6. Republish your Carrd site.  7. Open your site on your phone. Scroll to the form. Confirm it loads and looks clean.

build.title: Stage 4.  Test the full automation (10 minutes)
build.instructions: 1. On your phone, fill the form with a real name and a real email you can check.  2. Hit submit.  3. Open your email. The auto-confirmation should arrive within thirty seconds.  4. Read the email. Does it sound like you? If not, edit and republish.  5. Open Tally on your laptop. Check the submission appears in the dashboard.  6. Confirm you received the notification email too.  7. Now you have three confirmations of one automated workflow. Take a screenshot of the Tally dashboard with your test submission for your portfolio.

build.title: Stage 5.  Save the proof (5 minutes)
build.instructions: The agent is invisible by design. Your portfolio needs evidence it exists. Two things to capture. The Tally form share URL (visible work). A screenshot of the Tally dashboard showing your test submission (proof it runs).

test_output.title: The live test
test_output.description: You filled your own form. The confirmation email arrived in under thirty seconds.

test_output.title: The personal test
test_output.description: The confirmation email reads like a person wrote it. Not templated. Not generic.

test_output.title: The reliability test
test_output.description: Three test submissions in a row, three confirmations. No failures.

test_output.title: The notification test
test_output.description: You receive a notification when someone submits.

test_output.title: The value test
test_output.description: You can name in one sentence what specifically happens for the user every time someone submits.

output_capture.guidance: This is the share URL Tally gave you after publishing your form. The portfolio will show this alongside a note that it triggers an automated workflow.

sessions.title: Build the Agent

sessions.set_the_stage:
You have built six things. Every single one was built on assumptions. You assumed you understood the problem. You assumed your user would find the interface clear. You assumed the output was valuable enough to act on.

Some of those assumptions are right. Some are wrong. And until now you did not know which is which.

Over lunch you put what you built in front of a real person who has the problem. Not a friend who wants to support you. Someone who genuinely has the problem and has no reason to be kind. You took verbatim notes. Now you synthesise.

prompts.title: Synthesise what you heard
prompts.content:
I just had a validation conversation. Here is what I heard.

What they said about the problem: [verbatim notes]
What they said about the current workaround: [verbatim notes]
Their reaction when they saw the product: [what they did and said]
What they said about the output: [verbatim notes]
Their answer to the payment question: [verbatim notes]
The one thing they said that I did not expect: [verbatim notes]

Analyse what I heard through three lenses.

Humanics, what did this conversation reveal about my target user that I did not know before?
Mechanics, what did this reveal about how the product actually works versus how I assumed it worked?
Valuenomics, what did this reveal about the real value of the product?

End with three specific changes I should make based on what I heard.

build.title: Synthesise alone (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Run the prompt with your verbatim notes. Read Claude's synthesis carefully. Pay attention to anything Claude flags that you did not see yourself.

build.title: Cohort sync (20 minutes)
build.instructions: The room comes together. Three questions, everyone writes their answer before sharing. What is the most surprising thing you heard? What did your validation target say that you wrote down in their exact words? What are you changing because of this conversation?

build.title: Map the patterns
build.instructions: Three people share each answer. The facilitator maps patterns across the cohort. The patterns that appear in multiple participants are the most important learnings of the entire program.

test_output.title: The conversation test
test_output.description: You had a real conversation with a real person who genuinely has the problem. Not a friend.

test_output.title: The language test
test_output.description: You can name three specific words or phrases your validation target used that were different from the words you have been using.

test_output.title: The surprise test
test_output.description: You can name one specific thing your validation target said that genuinely surprised you. If you cannot, the conversation was too comfortable.

test_output.title: The payment test
test_output.description: You asked whether they would pay. You got a clear answer.

test_output.title: The change test
test_output.description: You can name three specific changes you will make based on what you heard.

sessions.title: Validation Synthesis
sessions.break_note: Stretch, water, fresh air. Back in fifteen.

sessions.set_the_stage:
Up to this point you have been building under time pressure. This hour is the only one in the workshop that is not. Use it deliberately.

Three things might want your attention. Choose the one that matters most for your specific build. Do not flit between three things. Pick one and give it the full hour.

build.title: Option 1.  Apply what you learned from validation
build.instructions: If your validation conversation surfaced something genuinely useful, make one substantial change based on it. Not three changes. The one that most directly addresses what your target user told you.

build.title: Option 2.  Fix what is fragile
build.instructions: Open every link in your build chain. Carrd site. AI App URL. Chatbot. Audio. Video. Tally form. Run through the journey as a stranger would. Anything fragile, broken, or confusing, fix it now. You do not want it failing during your demo.

build.title: Option 3.  Prepare for the demo
build.instructions: Ninety minutes of demo time is coming. Each participant has four minutes. Use this hour to rehearse what you will say, in what order, and what you will show. Run through your demo at least twice, ideally with someone watching.

sessions.title: Final Polish and Demo Day prep

sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at this room. It looks different from this morning. That is intentional.

What is about to happen is different from everything that came before it. For the last two days you have been building. Heads down. Focused on the product. Now you shift. From builder to founder.

A founder does not just build things. A founder can stand in front of people who do not know them, who have not been on this journey, who have no reason to be impressed, and make them understand in four minutes why what they built matters.

That is what Demo Day is. Not a show and tell. Not a progress update. A demonstration to real human beings that something real exists and that it creates real value.

Four minutes each. Strictly timed.

questions.title: What is the problem in one sentence, said to a stranger?
questions.body: Not the version you have been saying in this room all day. The version a complete stranger would understand in ten seconds.

questions.title: What is the sequence of your six modalities?
questions.body: AI App first. Website second. Chatbot embedded on the website third. Audio fourth. Video fifth. Agent sixth. By the end, the cohort sees not just a product but a complete solopreneur business.

questions.title: What is your impressive moment?
questions.body: The single moment during the four minutes when you want the room to feel that is genuinely good. Do not say and here is the impressive part. Just show it. Pause. Let the room react.

questions.title: What do you say when something does not work?
questions.body: Two acceptable responses. Narrate it. In the final version this will produce a specific output. What you are seeing is a limitation I identified during validation. Or pivot. Move to the next modality without dwelling and come back at the end. What is not acceptable is apologising repeatedly or abandoning the structure.

questions.title: What is the one specific next action you commit to in the Build Month?
questions.body: Not three things. Not a plan. One thing. A vague commitment signals unclear thinking. A specific commitment signals genuine learning.

prompts.title: Structure the demo
prompts.content:
I am about to present at Demo Day. I have four minutes. I have six modalities to show. Help me structure the presentation.

My product: [one sentence solution]
My target user: [human portrait]
My problem statement for the demo: [from Question 1]
My impressive moment: [from Question 3]
What did not work perfectly today: [from Question 4]
My one specific next action: [from Question 5]

Give me the exact structure for my four-minute demo.

Opening, thirty seconds. The problem statement. Spoken directly to the audience. No product yet.
Modality sequence, three minutes. Six modalities in order. For each one, one sentence of narration before showing it. What to say during the impressive moment. What to say if something does not work.
Closing, thirty seconds. What I learned from validation. My one specific next action. Said as a commitment, not a plan.

Make the opening and closing memorable. The audience will remember the first thing they hear and the last thing they hear.

build.title: Section 1.  The problem (30 seconds)
build.instructions: Spoken directly to the audience. Not read from notes. One sentence on the problem. One sentence on who has it. One number, what it costs them. Then silence. Two seconds. Let the problem land before showing the solution.

build.title: Section 2.  The live product (3 minutes)
build.instructions: Open the AI App. Enter a real input. Show the output. Pause on the impressive moment. Move to the website. Show the headline. Click through to the AI App from the call-to-action button. Show the chatbot. Type one real question. Show the response. Play the audio for fifteen seconds. Play the first fifteen seconds of the video from LinkedIn. Show the Tally form. Submit a test entry. Show the auto-confirmation arrive.

build.title: Section 3.  What is next (30 seconds)
build.instructions: One thing learned from validation. Spoken in the validation target's exact words. One specific change committed to for the Build Month. Named precisely. One sentence close. Not a thank you. A statement of intent.

build.title: After each demo.  The cohort responds (60 seconds)
build.instructions: Every participant writes one word or one short phrase. Simultaneously. Then shares. Not feedback. An impression. Compelling. Needed. Confused. Want this. The facilitator reads the pattern across responses.

test_output.title: The live product test
test_output.description: Every modality worked during your demo. AI App. Website CTA. Chatbot. Audio. Video. Agent.

test_output.title: The stranger test
test_output.description: Someone who knew nothing about your product before the demo can describe what it does, who it is for, and what makes it valuable.

test_output.title: The impressive moment test
test_output.description: You paused after the impressive moment. The room reacted.

test_output.title: The honesty test
test_output.description: You acknowledged at least one thing not yet perfect and connected it to what you learned from validation.

test_output.title: The commitment test
test_output.description: You named one specific next action, specific enough that the cohort could hold you accountable in four weeks.

sessions.title: Internal Demo Day

sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop for a moment. Look at what you have in front of you right now. Forty-eight hours ago this did not exist. None of it. Not the product. Not the website. Not the chatbot. Not the audio. Not the video. Not the agent. Not the portfolio you just saw.

You came in with an idea on a card. You leave with six live things in the world, collected into one URL.

This hour is about what you do with that URL next.

Most of this hour goes to the placement door. The LinkedIn post you write. The recruiters you message. The interviews you start lining up. The 90-second demo you already know how to run. Your portfolio is ready. Use it.

At the end of this hour, for those who want it, we open the second door. Orange Caterpillar runs a four-week structured program for builders who want to grow what they built into a real micro-venture. Not everyone applies. The ones who do are the ones who could not stop building if they tried.

questions.title: What is the one thing you learned this weekend that you did not expect to learn?
questions.body: Not what you expected to learn. The one thing that surprised you. Everyone answers in sequence around the room. One sentence each.

questions.title: Who are the first three people who will see your portfolio URL this week?
questions.body: Not categories. Names. A recruiter you have been talking to. A hiring manager whose role you want. An alumnus from your college doing something interesting. If you cannot name three real people, you do not have a placement plan yet, you have a wish.

questions.title: What is the one role or company you most want to land?
questions.body: Be specific. Not great companies. One name. The one that would make this whole weekend feel like the best decision of the semester. Write it down.

questions.title: What is the strongest thing you built this weekend that proves you can do that role?
questions.body: Of your six builds, which one most directly demonstrates the skill that role needs? When you send your portfolio URL, that is the build you point to first.

questions.title: What would success in four weeks look like for you?
questions.body: Not the program's definition. Yours. Three interviews lined up. One offer on the table. A direct message back from someone you admire. Name it precisely.

hmv.dimension: Humanics in the next four weeks
techniques: One real conversation per week. Minimum. With a recruiter, a hiring manager, or an alumnus.
Personal messages outperform applications. One LinkedIn message with your portfolio URL beats five generic applications.
If a recruiter or founder responds, you reply within twelve hours. Speed signals seriousness.

hmv.dimension: Mechanics in the next four weeks
techniques: The most common failure is waiting for the portfolio to be ready before applying. It is ready. Apply.
Every change to your portfolio should be traceable to feedback from a real recruiter or user.
When you feel the urge to rebuild before applying, ask. Am I rebuilding because it is fundamentally wrong, or because I am uncomfortable showing something imperfect?

hmv.dimension: Valuenomics in the next four weeks
techniques: Week 1. Send your portfolio URL to three specific people you have already identified.
Week 2. One LinkedIn post and three direct messages to roles you actually want.
Week 3. Refine the 90-second demo based on questions you got in your first interviews.
Week 4. Decide. Is the portfolio earning you what you want, or do you need to change the message?

prompts.title: Write your LinkedIn launch post
prompts.content:
I just finished the Just Build workshop and have a portfolio of six AI-powered builds. I need a LinkedIn launch post.

My focus or strongest build: [one sentence, from Question 4]
My target user or problem: [one sentence]
The one thing I built that surprised me: [from Question 1]
My portfolio URL: [from the portfolio reveal]
The kind of role I want next: [from Question 3]

Write me a LinkedIn post that.

Opens with the most surprising or impressive thing about what I built.
Names the problem I was solving in one sentence, in plain language.
Lists what I built, six things, in a clean and scannable format.
Ends with my portfolio URL and a specific invitation. If you are hiring for AI-adjacent roles, I would love to chat.

Keep it under 200 words. Conversational. Honest. Not boastful. Sound like a human who built something, not a marketer.

prompts.title: Draft your three outreach messages
prompts.content:
I need to send my portfolio URL to three specific people this week. Help me draft a personal message for each.

Person 1, the recruiter or hiring manager.
Name, role, why I want to reach them: [name and one sentence]

Person 2, the alumnus from my college.
Name, role, why I want to reach them: [name and one sentence]

Person 3, the founder or builder I admire who is publicly active.
Name, role, why I want to reach them: [name and one sentence]

My portfolio URL: [from the portfolio reveal]

For each person, write me a message under 80 words. Personal. Specific. Not generic. Reference something they have actually said or done that you noticed. End with my portfolio URL and a clear, low-friction ask. A 15-minute call this week. A reply. A single question they would be willing to answer.

build.title: Stage 1.  Write the LinkedIn launch post (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Paste Prompt 1 into Claude. Read the post out loud. Edit anything that sounds like a marketer. Do not post tonight. We post tomorrow morning after one night of distance. Save the draft somewhere you will find it. The post lives on your LinkedIn for years. Get it right.

build.title: Stage 2.  Draft the three outreach messages (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Paste Prompt 2 into the same Claude conversation. Use the three names you wrote down for Question 2. Read each message out loud. If it sounds copy-pasted from a template, ask Claude to make it more specific. Save all three messages for tomorrow.

build.title: Stage 3.  Public commitments (15 minutes)
build.instructions: Everyone stands. Each person states three things to the room. The one role or company they most want to land. The three real people they will message before Friday. The one thing they will not change about their portfolio while applying. Vague commitments get gently pushed back on in real time. Specific commitments get noted.

build.title: Stage 4.  The four-week structure (10 minutes)
build.instructions: Weekly group session, same day, same time, every week, ninety minutes. Office hours twice per week, thirty minutes each, bookable. The WhatsApp alumni group for sharing recruiter conversations, interview prep, and wins. You are not on your own. The cohort is the structure.

build.title: Stage 5.  The optional next door (5 minutes)
build.instructions: Some of you cannot stop thinking about the idea you built. The product feels real. The validation conversation lit something up. If that is you, the Orange Caterpillar Micro-Venture Program is open for applications. Four weeks of structured iteration, weekly reviews, and a real Demo Day to investors and operators. Applications close one week after this workshop. Apply at justbuild.orangecaterpillar.com/apply. The program is selective. We accept the builders who showed real momentum this weekend. Talk to a facilitator if you want to know more.

test_output.title: The post test
test_output.description: You have a LinkedIn launch post drafted, edited, and ready to publish tomorrow morning.

test_output.title: The outreach test
test_output.description: You have three personal messages drafted, one for each of three real people you will send to this week.

test_output.title: The role test
test_output.description: You can name in one sentence the role or company you most want to land. Specific. Real.

test_output.title: The commitment test
test_output.description: You stated three specific commitments to the room. The role. The three people. The thing you will not change.

test_output.title: The application test
test_output.description: If the micro-venture path is calling you, you know where the OC Micro-Venture Program application lives.

sessions.title: Close and what comes next
